text: The surgeon places a liver retractor to lift the liver off the stomach, revealing a decompressed liver due to preoperative weight loss. The omentum is rolled up towards the head to locate the ligament of Treitz, which comes into view.

The robotic instruments are docked, with a Cartier grasper visible on the right side of the screen handling the intestine. The surgeon measures the biliary limb approximately twenty centimeters downstream from the ligament of Treitz. The assisting surgeon performs blunt dissection on the small bowel mesentery to ensure it is lengthy and flexible enough to reach the upper abdomen. A robot stapler is introduced into the surgical field.

The robotic stapler divides the small intestine, separating the biliary limb on the left from the roux limb on the right, which will later attach to the gastric pouch. The harmonic scalpel is used to divide the mesentery, allowing the roux limb to reach the upper abdomen. Steam condensation and small tissue fragments create a snow-like appearance on the screen.

The surgeon measures the Roux limb to 150 centimeters downstream, with the common channel visible on the right and the biliary limb held by the grasper on the left. A skid mark is noted on the biliary limb, which will be incorporated into the anastomosis to prevent leakage. The surgeon uses a Vicryl suture to align the small bowel for anastomosis, demonstrating the flexibility of robot instruments in suturing. Two alignment sutures are placed before using the harmonic scalpel to create the enterotomies for a single-layer hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies using a harmonic scalpel, each approximately one centimeter in length, for a single-layer sutured anastomosis. The biliary limb is positioned on the left side of the screen, while the common channel is on the right, facilitating the mixing of biliary and pancreatic secretions with food downstream.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to place far corner sutures with Vicryl on the back wall of the anastomosis. The robotic technique allows precise needle placement, capturing tissue layers effectively. The surgeon performs a backhand stitch, demonstrating the flexibility and precision of robotic suturing.

The surgeon uses a Vicryl suture to secure the far corner of the single-layer jejunal anastomosis, utilizing the stereoscopic visualization and flexible robotic arms to precisely place the SH needle. The suture loop is pulled down to ensure proper tension, and the anastomosis is lined up for a secure, non-leaking connection. The back row is completed, and the front row is prepared for suturing.

The assistant sets aside the needle on the left side of the screen and delivers a 2-0 silk suture for closing the small bowel mesenteric defect. The surgeon uses robotic visualization to identify the back wall of the stomach, passing the Roux limb cephalad behind the colon into the retrogastric space. The colon and omentum are rolled towards the feet, revealing the stomach. The gastrohepatic ligament is opened widely to isolate the lesser curve neurovascular bundle, facilitating entry into the retrogastric space. The robotic stapler is used to fire a transverse blue load, aligning vertical staple loads to form the gastric pouch distal to the esophageal fat pad.

The surgeon identifies the anterior border of the left cruce and dissects the high posterior short gastric vessel to the patient's left using robotic instruments. The stomach is pulled down off the left cruce before placing the stapler. The vertical division of the gastric pouch is completed using robotic staple loads, with the final white staple load ensuring good compression and minimal staple line bleeding. The gastric segments are separated to form a small, isolated gastric pouch. The distal bypass stomach is moved to locate the roux limb in the retrogastric space, which is then brought up and supported by the screen right stapler arm for posterior alignment sutures. An orogastric tube is advanced to guide the placement of the posterior running suture, aligning the back wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the back wall alignment of the anastomosis and excises the gastric staple line to create a generous gastrojejunal anastomosis. This approach aims to minimize the risk of postoperative stricture formation by ensuring adequate perfusion and reducing tension at the anastomosis site.

The surgeon uses two robotic needle drivers to perform left-handed suturing on the gastrojejunostomy anastomosis. Vicryl sutures are placed at the far corner of the anastomosis, ensuring proper alignment and tension. The suturing technique is demonstrated with backhand stitches under clear visualization, creating a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the sutured approximation of the far corner of the gastric pouch using robotic assistance. The orogastric tube is advanced into the pouch and guided across the anastomosis into the roux limb for positioning before performing a methylene blue pressure test to check for leaks. The front wall of the anastomosis is reinforced with sutures, with small camera adjustments made for optimal visualization.

Dr. Alvarez uses a large Babcock instrument to occlude the outflow from the Roux limb, inflating it with methylene blue to check for leaks. The surgeon visually inspects the lesser and greater curved sides of the anastomosis for any signs of leakage. After confirming the integrity of the anastomosis, interrupted anti-tension sutures are placed at the corners and along the front wall using a 2-0 Vicryl suture. The procedure is performed in a reverse Trendelenburg position, ensuring ergonomic efficiency with robotic assistance.

The surgeon closes the Peterson defect using Tua silk sutures, aiming to create beneficial adhesions. Three interrupted sutures are placed to secure the Roux limb to the transverse mesocolon, preventing movement and potential herniation. The Roux limb serosa is sutured to the transverse mesocolon for strong fixation. The gastrojejunal anastomosis is inspected, showing no bleeding and proper positioning.
